← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

South Asia

Stupas, cave temples, bronze gods, and marble mausoleums.

Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, and Islamic traditions layered on top of each other for two millennia. Much of the greatest work is architecture carved straight out of living rock.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2500 BCE · Pakistan
A Bronze Age city with grid streets, covered drains, and a Great Bath. Its script is still undeciphered.
Harappa
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2600–1900 BCE · Pakistan
One of the two great Indus cities, grid-planned like Mohenjo-daro. Its granary, citadel, and burial mounds lie under centuries of British railway ballast quarried from the ruins.
Pashupati Seal
Indus Valley · c. 2350–2000 BCE
A steatite seal of a seated, horned figure surrounded by animals — sometimes read as a proto-Shiva, sometimes a shaman. We can't read the script that runs along the top.
Priest-King of Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley · c. 2000 BCE · Pakistan
A small bearded steatite bust, head-band and trefoil-patterned robe. Its title is a guess: we don't know if Mohenjo-daro had priests, kings, or this man at all.

1000 BCE – 0

Great Stupa at Sanchi
Mauryan → Shunga · 3rd c. BCE onward
A hemispherical mound containing relics of the Buddha. The four carved gateways around it narrate his life without ever depicting him directly.
Lion Capital of Ashoka
Mauryan · c. 250 BCE · Sarnath
Four back-to-back lions atop a polished sandstone column, once topped by a wheel. Adopted as the national emblem of India in 1950.
Rampurva Bull Capital
Mauryan · 3rd c. BCE · Bihar
An Ashokan pillar capital: a single polished Chunar sandstone bull, so highly burnished it was mistaken for metal. One of the finest survivals of the Mauryan court style.
Ajanta Caves
Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE – 6th c. CE · Maharashtra
Thirty rock-cut monasteries with surviving murals of the Buddha's past lives. Abandoned, forgotten, and rediscovered by a tiger-hunting British officer in 1819.
Bhaja Caves
Early Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE · Maharashtra
Twenty-two rock-cut viharas and a chaitya, with wooden ribs imitated in stone. Reliefs show figures riding elephants, dancing — a rare look at secular imagery in early Buddhist art.
Karla Caves
Early Buddhist · 1st c. BCE · Maharashtra
The largest rock-cut chaitya hall in India — a barrel-vaulted cave church with a lantern roof carved in stone to mimic wood beams it never had.

0 – 500 CE

Bhimbetka Rock Shelters
Mesolithic onward · 30,000 BCE – historical · India
Painted caves with some of the oldest hunting scenes on earth — a human riding a gigantic red animal, drawn in ochre and charcoal.
Amaravati Stupa Reliefs
Satavahana · 2nd c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Andhra Pradesh
A Buddhist stupa whose limestone casing was carved with some of the earliest Buddha figures in India. Most pieces are now in the British Museum and the Chennai Government Museum.
Mathura Bodhisattva
Kushan · 1st–3rd c. CE · Uttar Pradesh
Red sandstone figures in a distinctly Indian idiom — broad-shouldered, loose-robed, flatter and more frontal than the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school evolving to the northwest.
Gandhara Buddha
Greco-Buddhist · 1st–5th c. CE · Pakistan/Afghanistan
The first sculptural images of the Buddha, made in schist and draped in togas. A crossroads style born from Alexander's successors.
Sigiriya
Anuradhapura period · 477 CE · Sri Lanka
A palace on top of a 660-foot granite plug. Its western face once carried 500 paintings of celestial women; twenty-one still survive.

500 – 1000

Dhamek Stupa
Gupta era · 500 CE · Sarnath
Solid brick stupa marking the spot of the Buddha's first sermon. Its upper band of geometric floral carving is some of the finest Gupta ornament anywhere.
Dashavatara Temple, Deogarh
Gupta · c. 500 CE · Uttar Pradesh
One of the earliest surviving Hindu stone temples, a compact cube topped by a now-damaged tower. Its relief panels — Vishnu asleep on Ananta, Nara-Narayana, the rescue of the elephant king Gajendra — are foundational images of Hindu narrative art.
Swayambhunath
Newar · c. 500 CE · Kathmandu
The 'monkey temple' atop a hill in the Kathmandu Valley, reached by 365 steps flanked by polychrome Buddhas. A 2015 earthquake split several subsidiary shrines; the main stupa, its painted eyes impassive, rode out the shaking.
Buddhas of Bamiyan
Greco-Buddhist · 6th c. · Afghanistan
Two standing Buddhas, 38 and 55 meters tall, carved into a sandstone cliff on the Silk Road. The Taliban dynamited them in 2001; projection mapping now fills the empty niches on quiet nights.
Elephanta Caves
Kalachuri / Chalukya · 5th–8th c. · Mumbai
Rock-cut Shiva temple on an island. The Trimurti — Shiva as creator, preserver, and destroyer — emerges twenty feet tall from the living stone.
Jokhang Temple
Tibetan Buddhist · 642 CE · Lhasa
Tibet's holiest temple, founded by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo Shakyamuni statue brought by his Tang Chinese wife. Pilgrims complete three prostrations every few steps around the Barkhor circuit outside; the flagstones are polished to a mirror.
Pancha Rathas
Pallava · 7th c. · Mahabalipuram
Five monolithic shrines carved from a single granite ridge in the shape of temple chariots. Each is a different architectural experiment — the prototypes for a millennium of South Indian temples.
Pattadakal
Chalukya dynasty · 7th–8th c. · Karnataka
A proving ground for Indian temple architecture — northern and southern styles built side by side for direct comparison before being exported across the subcontinent.
Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
Pallava · c. 725 CE · Tamil Nadu
One of the earliest stone temples in South India. It stands on the Bay of Bengal; the 2004 tsunami briefly receded far enough to reveal more submerged temples.
Kailasa Temple, Ellora
Rashtrakuta · c. 760 CE · Maharashtra
The largest monolithic sculpture on earth — a full Dravida-style temple carved top-down from a single basalt cliff, freeing perhaps two hundred thousand tonnes of rock. Krishna I's masons worked to a plan no draft of which survives; many believe none existed.
Samye Monastery
Tibetan Buddhist · 779 CE · U-Tsang
The first monastery built in Tibet, laid out as a three-dimensional mandala with the central temple representing Mount Meru and four chapels for the cosmic continents. Its founder Trisong Detsen imported Indian masters to ordain the first seven Tibetan monks.
Nalanda Mahavihara
Pala dynasty · 5th–12th c. · Bihar
A Buddhist university that drew ten thousand monks from across Asia. Burned by Bakhtiyar Khalji around 1193; the library, they say, smoked for months.
Chand Baori
Gurjara-Pratihara · 9th c. · Rajasthan
A thirteen-story stepwell carved with 3,500 narrow steps into dizzying geometric patterns down its walls. One of the oldest and deepest stepwells in India.
Gommateshwara Statue at Shravanabelagola
by Aristanemi
Ganga dynasty · 983 CE · Karnataka
A 17-meter monolithic nude of the Jain ascetic Bahubali, absorbed in meditation so deep that vines have grown up his legs. Every twelve years, pilgrims anoint the figure with milk, turmeric, and sandalwood in the Mahamastakabhisheka festival.

1000 – 1500

Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of Dance)
Chola dynasty · 10th–11th c. · Tamil Nadu
Shiva dances the universe into being and out of it again, surrounded by a ring of flame, trampling the demon of ignorance.
Khajuraho Temples
Chandela dynasty · 950–1050 · Madhya Pradesh
Hindu and Jain temples covered in dense sculpture — gods, battles, and the frank erotic scenes the site is best known for.
Chola Bronzes
Chola dynasty · 9th–13th century · Tamil Nadu
Hollow-cast bronze icons of Shiva, Vishnu, and their consorts, produced by the lost-wax method for temple processions. The dynasty's patronage pushed Indian metal sculpture to its aesthetic high point — even today casters in Swamimalai work to thousand-year-old Chola templates.
Brihadeeswarar Temple
Chola dynasty · 1010 · Thanjavur
A granite tower 216 feet high, capped with a single 80-ton stone. A thousand years old and still in daily ritual use.
Gangaikonda Cholapuram
Chola · 1025 · Tamil Nadu
Rajendra Chola built this temple to commemorate his invasion of the Gangetic plain, bringing back a pot of Ganges water poured into the sanctum. The vimana is a softer, more feminine echo of his father's Brihadeeswarar at Thanjavur.
Modhera Sun Temple
Chaulukya dynasty · 1026 · Gujarat
Temple to Surya oriented so the first rays of the equinox sun strike the inner sanctum. A cistern called Ramakunda in front holds over a hundred miniature shrines along its steps.
Rani ki Vav
Chaulukya dynasty · 11th c. · Gujarat
A subterranean stepwell dug by Queen Udayamati for her husband. Seven levels down, 500 sculptures of Vishnu avatars and apsaras. Silted over by the Saraswati; rediscovered in the 1980s.
Dilwara Temples
Jain · 11th–13th c. · Mount Abu
White marble carved so finely the columns look more like lace than stone. Every surface is covered — floor, ceiling, arch.
Gal Vihara
Polonnaruwa · 12th c. · Sri Lanka
Four colossal Buddhas carved from a single granite wall — seated, standing, reclining, meditating. The face of the reclining Buddha is nine meters long.
Jagannath Temple, Puri
Eastern Ganga · 12th c. · Odisha
Home of the annual Rath Yatra — giant wooden chariots hauled through the streets carrying the three deities. The temple's cooks feed the god daily from one of the largest kitchens in the world.
Hoysaleswara Temple
Hoysala · 1150 · Halebidu, Karnataka
Carved from chloritic schist, which hardens after quarrying, enabling the Hoysala sculptors to cut almost impossibly thin details. Elephants, musicians, and gods wrap the plinth in horizontal registers that add up to miles of relief never repeating itself.
Airavatesvara Temple
Chola · 1166 · Darasuram, Tamil Nadu
Rajaraja II's jewel-box Shiva temple, its front mandapa shaped like a chariot pulled by stone horses. The plinth is carved with musicians, dancers, and — charmingly — optical-illusion animals: a bull that doubles as an elephant depending on which end you start from.
Konark Sun Temple
Eastern Ganga dynasty · 13th c. · Odisha
A massive stone chariot for the sun god, pulled by seven horses, wheels carved to tell the time from their shadows.
Chennakeshava Temple, Somanathapura
Hoysala · 1268 · Karnataka
A star-plan temple with three shrines on a single platform, surrounded by a cloistered courtyard. Every surface is carved: gods in scalloped niches, musicians in frothing bands, a frieze of marching elephants at ground level supporting the whole weight of the building.
Thangka Painting
Tibetan Buddhist · c. 11th century onward · Himalayan region
Scroll paintings on cotton or silk, bordered with brocade, depicting deities in complex mandalic geometries for visualization practice. Pigments are ground from powdered turquoise, cinnabar, and malachite — gold highlights are burnished with a dog's tooth.
Hampi (Vijayanagara)
Vijayanagara Empire · 14th–16th c. · Karnataka
Capital of the last great Hindu empire in the south. Sacked in 1565; the boulder-strewn ruins sprawl over 16 square miles.
Ranakpur Jain Temple
Jain · 15th c. · Rajasthan
1,444 carved marble columns, no two alike. The complex is aligned so that the sun reaches the central sanctum for only a few days a year.
Mehrangarh Fort
Rathore dynasty · 1459 onward · Jodhpur
A hundred-meter cliff-top fort in the Blue City of Jodhpur. Its walls still bear handprints of the widows of maharajas who threw themselves on the pyre in the medieval practice of sati.

1500 – 1800

Boudhanath Stupa
Newar/Tibetan Buddhist · 15th century · Kathmandu
One of the largest stupas in Asia, thirty-six meters across, its dome topped by the painted eyes of the Buddha watching in four directions. Circumambulated daily by Tibetan refugees, pilgrims, and locals — prayer-wheel grooves are worn smooth into the granite.
Virupaksha Temple, Hampi
Vijayanagara · 7th century onward · Karnataka
The only temple in Hampi still in continuous worship after the sack of 1565. Its nine-tiered gopura pours down layers of painted deities; in the courtyard a pinhole in the outer wall projects the gopura inverted onto the opposite wall — a Vijayanagara camera obscura.
Lepakshi Temple
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Andhra Pradesh
A granite temple to Shiva whose ceiling holds the largest surviving Vijayanagara mural. One of its columns hangs a fraction of an inch above the floor — pilgrims pass cloth beneath it.
Lotus Mahal
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Hampi
A pavilion in the zenana quarter with lotus-shaped scalloped arches. Architectural hybrid: Hindu temple superstructure atop Islamic multifoil arches — courtly syncretism in stone.
Humayun's Tomb
Mughal · 1570 · Delhi
The first garden tomb of the Mughals and the prototype for the Taj Mahal eighty years later. A red sandstone dome in a four-quartered paradise garden.
Fatehpur Sikri
Mughal · Akbar · 1571 · Uttar Pradesh
A red sandstone capital built and abandoned in fifteen years when the water ran out. The ghost city of the greatest of the Mughals.
Meenakshi Temple
Nayak period · 16th–17th c. · Madurai
Fourteen gateway towers covered in thousands of painted figures. The whole complex is repainted every twelve years.
Akbar's Tomb
Mughal · 1605–1613 · Sikandra, Agra
A five-story mausoleum on a platform in a paradise garden. Akbar designed much of it himself; Jahangir's later changes produced the strange tiered result.
Punakha Dzong
Bhutan · 1637 · Punakha
Bhutan's second dzong, built at the confluence of two rivers and still the winter home of the monastic body. Its five-story central utse leans out over the water; inside, eighteenth-century murals of the Buddha's life glow in whale-oil lamplight.
Taj Mahal
Mughal · 1632–1653 · Agra
A tomb Shah Jahan built for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. The marble is inlaid with 28 kinds of gem and stone.
Red Fort
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1648 · Delhi
Red sandstone walls two kilometers around. Inside, the Diwan-i-Khas held the Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah of Persia took it to Isfahan in 1739.
Amber Fort
Rajput · 16th–18th c. · Jaipur
Hilltop fort-palace of the Kachhwaha dynasty. The Hall of Mirrors inside glitters with thousands of convex glass inlays; a single candle lights the whole room.
Mughal Miniature Painting
Mughal · 16th–18th c. · North India
Album leaves commissioned by the Mughal emperors — historical chronicles, portraits, and natural history drawn with jeweller's precision.
Gol Gumbaz
Adil Shahi · 1656 · Bijapur
Tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah. The dome is the second-largest in the world, and the whispering gallery underneath it repeats a voice seven times.
Jama Masjid, Delhi
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1656 · Delhi
The largest mosque in India — a red sandstone and marble courtyard for 25,000. Built in the same decade as the Taj Mahal, by the same emperor.
Patan Durbar Square
Malla dynasty · 17th century · Lalitpur, Nepal
The royal plaza of the Malla kings of Patan, edged with Newar pagodas and a stone Krishna Mandir carved in imitation of north Indian shikharas. Several temples collapsed in 2015 and have been reconstructed with timber dowels rather than steel.
Paro Taktsang (Tiger's Nest Monastery)
Bhutan · 1692 · Paro
Perched on a cliff 900 meters above the Paro valley, on the ledge where Guru Rinpoche was said to have meditated for three years, three months, three weeks, three days, and three hours. A 1998 fire destroyed most of it; the rebuild faithfully repeats the original timber joinery.
Golden Temple
Sikh · 16th–19th c. · Amritsar
The holiest site of Sikhism. Marble walls inlaid with gemstone, upper floors covered in gold leaf, the whole thing floating on a sacred pool.
Thanjavur Painting
Nayaka / Maratha · 16th–19th c. · Tamil Nadu
Devotional paintings built up in gesso, then layered in gold leaf, semi-precious stones, and mirrored glass. Surface so encrusted the figures seem to float off the board.
Jantar Mantar
Rajput · Jai Singh II · 1734 · Jaipur
An observatory of huge masonry instruments — sundials ninety feet tall, instruments to locate any star. Built to correct the drifting calendars of Mughal India.
Hawa Mahal
Rajput · 1799 · Jaipur
The "Palace of Winds" — a honeycomb facade of 953 latticed windows, built so the women of the court could watch the street without being seen.

1800 onward

Mahabodhi Temple
Gupta → restored 19th c. · Bodh Gaya
Built at the spot the Buddha attained enlightenment under a Bodhi tree. A direct descendant of that tree still grows beside the temple.
Mysore Palace
Indo-Saracenic · 1912 · Karnataka
The current incarnation — fourth to stand on the site — is a wedding cake of Hindu, Mughal, Rajput, and Gothic elements. Lit up nightly by nearly a hundred thousand bulbs.
Victoria Memorial
British Raj · 1906–1921 · Kolkata
A white marble domed memorial to Queen Victoria, built at the height of imperial self-regard. Now a museum of the Raj and the nationalism that ended it.
Somnath Temple
Chaulukya → rebuilt 1951 · Gujarat
A Hindu pilgrimage site destroyed and rebuilt seven times — plundered most famously by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1025. The current temple was built after Indian independence, at the sea's edge.
Rock Garden of Chandigarh
by Nek Chand
Outsider · Nek Chand · 1957 onward · India
A secret sculpture park a road inspector built on the sly from broken bangles, electric switches, ceramic shards, and urban trash. Discovered by authorities in 1975; preserved on public demand.
Lotus Temple
by Fariborz Sahba
Modernist Bahá'í · Fariborz Sahba · 1986 · Delhi
A Bahá'í House of Worship in 27 free-standing white marble petals arranged in three clusters — one of the most visited buildings in the world.
←Islamic World East Asia→
Images via Wikimedia Commons. Click any image to open it and see its source.
